title: List of Thai ingredients
rows: Bai makok is the leaf of the Spondias mombin , a relative of the cashew . The young leaves are served raw with certain types of Nam phrik ( Thai chilli pastes ) . The taste is sour and slightly bitter . The fruit of this tree are also eaten

Young feathery leaves of the Acacia pennata tree which are used in omelettes , soups and curries . In Northern Thai cuisine they are also eaten raw as for instance with Tam mamuang , a green mango salad
จิกน้ำ or กระโดน
Shoots , young leaves and flowers of the tree are eaten raw with Nam phrik . Popular in Isan
Can be eaten in Yam preparations , said to have medicinal value as well
Can be eaten raw or fried , but mostly it is used to make a blue food colouring to colour rice or sweets , like Khanom dok anchan
The flowers of the Sesbania grandiflora are often eaten steamed with Nam phrik or used in certain curries such as kaeng som
The flowers are usually eaten sauteed or in kaeng som
ดอกแคหัวหมู
Often confused with Dok khae thale , as both are also known as Dok khae pa . The flowers are usually eaten sauteed or in kaeng som
Mostly either boiled and eaten with Nam phrik or stir-fried in Phat dok salit
These small yellow flowers are eaten stir-fried , in omelette or in sweets such as in Khanom dok sano

Banana flowers can be eaten raw , e.g . Yam hua pli ( a spicy salad with thinly sliced banana flowers ) , or steamed with a Nam phrik ( chilli dip ) . It can also feature in Som tam , in soups or deep-fried , as in Thot man huapli . The taste of the steamed flowers is somewhat similar to that of artichokes

Literally translated , the Thai name means claws of the Garuda . These slightly bitter and slightly sour leaves can be served raw together with a chilli dip . It is also used as a vegetable in certain Thai curries
Commonly made into an omelet . Associated with Southern Thai cuisine
The young , slightly bitter leaves of the Ficus virens are used boiled in certain Northern Thai curries
Leaves and young pods are eaten raw . The large mature pods are grilled and the inside is scraped and eaten along with Lap
The leaves and flowers of the neem tree ( Azadirachta indica ) are eaten blanched , often with Nam phrik
This leaf is used raw as a wrapper for the Thai dish Miang kham

description: This table provides a list of Thai ingredients, detailing their Thai names, Thai script representations, English names, and descriptions of their uses in Thai cuisine. It serves as a reference for understanding the cultural and culinary significance of these ingredients.
